title: Bringing Up Buster

jokes.text: Narrator introduces cornballs as a Bluth family favorite since George Sr. unsuccessfully tried to market the device in the mid-70s.
jokes.setup: Narrator frames the cornball ritual as a wholesome family tradition.
jokes.punchline: The device was a failed commercial product George Sr. tried to market — immediately undercutting the nostalgia with failure.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator move — establishes wholesome warmth and immediately punctures it with Bluth incompetence. Sets up the running Cornballer gag economically. Character-specific because George Sr.'s failed schemes are his defining trait.

jokes.text: George Michael burns himself on the Cornballer: 'Son of a bitch!' / 'You plopped it!' / 'I'll plop you!'
jokes.setup: Narrator warmly describes a ritual Michael shares with his son before their bike ride.
jokes.punchline: The ritual immediately results in burns and comedic bickering — 'I'll plop you!' as a mock-threatening non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: 'I'll plop you!' works because of its absurd non-sequitur logic — it mirrors 'I'll plop it' but applied to a person, which is nonsensical. The slapstick is earned by the device's established danger.

jokes.text: Lindsay to Michael about George Michael: 'Or, you could not eat the cornballs and let your kid lead his own life.'
jokes.setup: Michael says they need to 'go extra hard to work off these cornballs.'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay offers the sane alternative — don't eat them — but frames it as enlightened parenting advice, revealing her lazy parenting philosophy.
jokes.explanation: Sharp economy — Lindsay's response is technically correct but reveals her as a negligent parent who dresses avoidance up as wisdom. The joke works on two levels: literal cornball advice and meta-commentary on her parenting.

jokes.text: Michael begins the proverb 'It's the poor carpenter that blames his shoddy tools for the...' and immediately burns himself on the Cornballer.
jokes.setup: Michael starts quoting a proverb about not blaming your tools.
jokes.punchline: He's immediately injured by the very tool he's defending, mid-sentence.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke construction. The setup (proverb about not blaming tools) creates an expectation of pomposity, and the Cornballer burns Michael mid-sentence, making the tool prove itself dangerous and Michael's argument collapse in real time. The interruption IS the punchline. Structurally immaculate — 9/25 words in and the joke lands. Earns an 8+ because the irony is immediate, structural, and physically embodied.

jokes.text: Lindsay bought a dress on the company credit card. Michael: 'You're returning that.' Lindsay: 'But...' Michael: 'Get a job.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay tries to explain paying for her dress 'myself' — with the company credit card.
jokes.punchline: 'Get a job' — delivered as a curt dismissal, cutting off her protest before it starts.
jokes.explanation: Works through economy and character — Michael's two-word dismissal is maximally efficient. Lindsay's 'But...' makes us anticipate a justification that never comes. Character-specific: this exchange perfectly encapsulates their dynamic.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Hey, the Cornballer. I thought these things were only legal in Mexico.' Narrator: 'In fact, the Cornballer wasn't legal anywhere. But George Sr. continued to market it there successfully. [cut to George Sr. in Mexico] Muy delicioso. Es verdad.'
jokes.setup: Gob naively says the Cornballer is 'only legal in Mexico.'
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals it's illegal everywhere, then cuts to George Sr. marketing it in Mexico anyway — with George Sr. speaking terrible Spanish to approving locals.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke that does several things simultaneously: Gob's confident ignorance, the narrator's corrective deadpan, and the cut to George Sr. exploiting a legal gray area in a foreign country. 'Muy delicioso / Es verdad' is perfectly absurd. This is AD at its densest — three jokes stacked in ten seconds. Justifies 8+ because of the structural elegance of the Gob setup/narrator correction/visual cutaway triple combo.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I had another big fight with Marta.' Narrator explains Marta is a Spanish-language soap opera star. Gob: 'It looks like I'm going to be staying here for a while.' Michael: 'No, it doesn't. It doesn't look like that.'
jokes.setup: Gob announces he'll be staying, as if it's an obvious conclusion.
jokes.punchline: Michael flatly refuses the premise twice — 'No, it doesn't. It doesn't look like that.'
jokes.explanation: The doubling of 'No, it doesn't. It doesn't look like that.' is a nice rhythmic touch — Michael correcting Gob's framing before dismissing the plan entirely. Standard but effective character-based sitcom exchange.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Maybe I should be more like you when it comes to parenting. No borders. No limits. Go ahead, touch the Cornballer. You know best.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just accused Michael of being overbearing with his son.
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically describes Lindsay's negligent parenting — then drops the Cornballer callback to illustrate it with perfect specificity.
jokes.explanation: The Cornballer callback here does double duty — it's a sharp callback to the physical comedy while also being the perfect specific example of 'no limits' parenting. 'You know best' as a sarcastic kicker landing on a small child burns himself is elegant. Strong character integration — only Michael would use this exact device to make this point.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'When I was a psychiatrist — and this is before I became an actor—' Michael: 'You're still not an actor.' Tobias: 'Lovely.'
jokes.setup: Tobias begins a psychiatry anecdote, reminding everyone of his failed acting career.
jokes.punchline: Michael bluntly corrects him — 'You're still not an actor' — and Tobias absorbs the insult with one resigned word: 'Lovely.'
jokes.explanation: 'Lovely' is perfect Tobias — theatrical, slightly British, utterly mismatched to the blunt humiliation he's just received. Michael's 'You're still not an actor' is maximally economical. Tobias's response shows he's absorbed the blow and moved on, which is funnier than protest. High character integration because 'Lovely' as a wounded retreat is pure Tobias Fünke.

jokes.text: Tobias burns himself on the Cornballer mid-psychiatric advice: 'Am I touching something? Hot! Hot, hot. Hot.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is delivering genuine psychiatric insight about overbearing fathers.
jokes.punchline: He touches the Cornballer, interrupting his own wisdom with a series of 'Hot! Hot, hot. Hot.' — a third Cornballer burn in the episode.
jokes.explanation: The third burn escalates the running gag and the comedy comes from the fact that Tobias is the THIRD person to burn himself on the same uncovered device in the same kitchen. The escalation is the point — nobody learns. Structurally, the interruption of his psychiatric advice is funnier than if he'd burned himself randomly.

jokes.text: Tobias, still recovering from the burn, pivots immediately: 'Now, take my daughter, for example. She lives her life and I get the pleasure of guessing what that might entail.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is advising Michael about connecting with his son.
jokes.punchline: He holds up his own relationship with Maeby as a positive example — but describes it as complete ignorance of her life, framed as a pleasure.
jokes.explanation: 'I get the pleasure of guessing what that might entail' is a lovely piece of self-deception — Tobias genuinely frames his parental neglect/obliviousness as a gift. The phrase 'guessing what that might entail' is beautifully constructed ignorance. High character integration.

jokes.text: Tobias demonstrates his hands-off parenting by asking Maeby where she's going, expecting her not to answer — but she does. 'I was totally wrong. She's reaching out to her actor daddy.'
jokes.setup: Tobias says he knows Maeby won't tell him where she's going — sets up audience expectation that she'll blow him off.
jokes.punchline: She answers honestly (going to audition for a play), and Tobias immediately reinterprets it as an emotional gesture toward him specifically — missing that it's a coincidence.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works on two levels: Tobias is wrong that she won't answer (she does), but ALSO wrong about why she's doing it (it's about a boy, not him). 'She's reaching out to her actor daddy' — Tobias can't stop making everything about his failed acting career. The narrator's immediate correction makes it a three-beat joke.

jokes.text: Now watch this. Maeby, where are you off to on this glorious Sunday afternoon? — [She won't tell me.] — I'm going to audition for a play. — Well, that time it didn't work.
jokes.setup: Tobias brags he doesn't need to know what Maeby is doing ('I get the pleasure of guessing') and demonstrates by asking her directly.
jokes.punchline: She actually tells him, forcing him to awkwardly admit 'Well, that time it didn't work' — his parenting philosophy demolished in seconds.
jokes.explanation: This is a surgical comedic demolition of a character's philosophy in under 20 seconds. Tobias sets up, performs, fails, and immediately revises with 'Well, that time it didn't work' — an admission so casual it doubles the laugh. The structure is perfect: the parenthetical '[She won't tell me]' implies this is how it usually goes, making the exception land harder. One of the tightest joke structures in the episode. 'Well, that time it didn't work' is extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Maeby is auditioning to get closer to Steve Holt; George Michael is also auditioning to get closer to his cousin Maeby. Both characters ask 'And then we actually get to kiss, right?' / 'There's a kiss, right?'
jokes.setup: The narrator explains that Maeby auditions to get closer to Steve Holt.
jokes.punchline: George Michael is doing the same thing — but to get closer to his COUSIN, which is immediately uncomfortable. Both of them ask about the kissing, which makes their respective motivations explicit and darkly parallel.
jokes.explanation: The parallel construction — Maeby auditions to kiss Steve Holt, George Michael auditions to kiss Maeby — is one of the show's defining running gags (the cousin crush). The parallelism of their identical question 'there's a kiss, right?' is elegant and darkly funny. Earns 8+ because the structure is formally perfect and the subject matter is delightfully transgressive.

jokes.text: Steve Holt! Steve Holt! — Steve Holt announces his own name (or others shout it) repeatedly at the audition results, including the stand-in posting: 'Stand-in for Steve Holt?'
jokes.setup: Cast list is posted after auditions.
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt's name is announced/celebrated with increasing enthusiasm — then immediately undercut by 'Stand-in for Steve Holt?' which deflates the energy.
jokes.explanation: Establishes 'Steve Holt!' as a running gag. The pattern of triumphant name + deflating follow-up ('Stand-in for Steve Holt?') is tight comedy. High quotability because 'Steve Holt!' becomes a show-level catchphrase. The escalation from two Holts to the stand-in punchline is efficient.

jokes.text: Lucille answers the phone and immediately says 'It'll fly out on its own. Why are you calling me? What do you need?' before Lindsay can say anything beyond hello.
jokes.setup: Lindsay calls her mother. 'Hi, Mama, it's Linds.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille, clearly dealing with a bird issue and annoyed, dispenses advice for an unseen problem ('It'll fly out on its own') and jumps straight to 'What do you need?' — zero warmth, total transactional efficiency.
jokes.explanation: Pure Lucille — she is so self-absorbed she can't even greet her daughter before pivoting to 'What do you need?' The 'it'll fly out on its own' advice she's giving the bird also functions as metaphorical advice for all of Lucille's children (let go). Strong character integration — this is only funny because it's Lucille.

jokes.text: Buster bursts in: 'It's a bird.' Lucille: 'I know it's a bird. I'm on the phone.'
jokes.setup: There's a bird somewhere in Lucille's apartment. Buster panics.
jokes.punchline: Lucille already knows — she's been managing it — and dismisses him with the phone call.
jokes.explanation: Quick and efficient. Buster's alarm vs. Lucille's total calm is a character contrast that works. 'I know it's a bird, I'm on the phone' is good deadpan economy. Not exceptional but solid.

jokes.text: Buster: 'It walked on my pillow.' Lucille doesn't care.
jokes.setup: Buster continues to panic about the bird.
jokes.punchline: 'It walked on my pillow' — implying Buster sleeps somewhere a bird could walk on his pillow, evoking a sad, childlike domestic situation. Lucille completely ignores this.
jokes.explanation: Small but effective — 'it walked on my pillow' hints at Buster's infantilized, possibly floor-level sleeping situation in his mother's home. The detail is funnier than the complaint itself. Character-specific to Buster's helplessness.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Michael just gave me the exact same lecture in front of everybody.' Lucille: 'Everybody? Who's everybody?' Lindsay lists everyone — and then the narrator delivers: 'He didn't invite Buster.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay mentions Michael lectured her in front of 'everybody.'
jokes.punchline: After listing everyone present (the kids, Gob, Tobias), the narrator adds that Buster wasn't invited — making Buster's exclusion from his own family's morning gathering the punchline.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's intervention makes this work — the comedy is in the TIMING of the reveal. Lucille's question ('Who's everybody?') briefly seems like it might lead to Buster being mentioned, but instead the narrator delivers the dark correction. This establishes the episode's Buster-as-forgotten-child theme economically and sadly. High craft for how much character and theme it packs into one line.

jokes.text: Lucille to Michael: 'You run around with everyone else, going on bike rides, making cornholes.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is chastising Michael for excluding Buster.
jokes.punchline: She says 'cornholes' instead of 'cornballs' — a genuine malapropism or deliberate Lucille vulgarity, either way absurd.
jokes.explanation: Whether Lucille genuinely doesn't know the word or says it without caring, 'cornholes' is perfectly wrong in the most Lucille way possible — oblivious to vulgarity, completely certain. A great malapropism that also serves as a callback to the Cornballer gag.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Everyone's laughing, riding and cornholing except Buster.'
jokes.setup: Continuation of Lucille's cornholes malapropism.
jokes.punchline: She doubles down: 'Everyone's laughing, riding and cornholing except Buster' — the rhythm of the sentence makes it even funnier, and 'cornholing' as a verb she uses with complete sincerity is absurd.
jokes.explanation: The doubling-down elevates it above joke 19. 'Everyone's laughing, riding and cornholing except Buster' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode — the tragic exclusion of Buster combined with the accidental obscenity creates perfect AD comedy. The sentence structure (three gerunds) gives it rhythm that makes the delivery even funnier. Earns 8+ — it's memorable, character-perfect, and the payoff of the malapropism setup.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I know he's not the sharpest knife in the drawer, but he's sensitive, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is defending Buster's honor to Michael.
jokes.punchline: She immediately qualifies his defense with 'I know he's not the sharpest knife in the drawer' — an admission of his intellectual limitations dressed up as a preamble to his defense.
jokes.explanation: Standard clichéd idiom used in a character-appropriate way — Lucille can't even defend Buster without insulting him first. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster overhears: 'That's cool. Mom packed me a change of clothes.' — Michael has just said 'This is not going to be a day at the beach.'
jokes.setup: Michael sets the terms: Buster can hang out but he has to work. 'This is not going to be a day at the beach.'
jokes.punchline: Buster enthusiastically says Mom packed him a change of clothes — interpreting everything as a beach day, having completely missed the point.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Michael's idiom 'not a day at the beach' is literal to Buster. It reveals Buster's obliviousness through a single line and requires no explanation. The 'Mom packed me a change of clothes' detail adds to the picture of Lucille treating Buster as a child preparing for an outing. Earns 8 for economy and character integration — only Buster could be this specifically obtuse.

jokes.text: Gob is in a bathrobe at the office. Michael: 'Gob, what are you doing here, and why are you in a bathrobe?' Gob: 'I camped out in the boardroom last night.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the Bluth Company office to find Gob in a bathrobe.
jokes.punchline: Gob has literally been living in the boardroom — homeless by choice after being kicked out of Michael's house.
jokes.explanation: Gob living in the boardroom is consistent with his entitled incompetence — he can't find shelter independently. The bathrobe is a good visual detail. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster is in his bathing suit at the office. Michael: 'why is he in his bathing suit?' Gob: 'Mom dropped me off to spend time with Michael.'
jokes.setup: Michael is questioning why Gob is in a bathrobe.
jokes.punchline: Buster is standing there in a bathing suit, having packed for the beach — directly paying off the 'Mom packed a change of clothes' joke from earlier.
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of the earlier joke — Buster ACTUALLY showed up in his bathing suit — is satisfying and escalates the Buster-as-child gag. The visual comedy of a grown man standing in a business office in swim trunks is strong.

jokes.text: Gob on Lucille: 'Spend time with Michael, or to serve her own menopausal needs? She's always got to wedge herself in the middle of us, so she can control everything.' Buster: 'Yeah. Mom's awesome.'
jokes.setup: Gob delivers a sophisticated psychological analysis of Lucille's controlling behavior.
jokes.punchline: Buster responds with cheerful sincerity: 'Yeah. Mom's awesome.' — completely missing and contradicting everything Gob just said.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Gob — of all people — gives a lucid, sophisticated critique of Lucille, only for Buster to immediately affirm the thing being criticized. 'Yeah. Mom's awesome.' is perfectly placed and perfectly delivered — the contrast between Gob's complexity and Buster's total absorption in Lucille is both hilarious and quietly tragic. High character integration: this could ONLY be Buster. 'Menopausal needs' is also a great throwaway. Earns 8+ for perfect character payoff and structural contrast.

jokes.text: Beat after Buster says 'Yeah. Mom's awesome.' — then a pause, then: 'Maybe we should call her.'
jokes.setup: Gob has explained Lucille's manipulation and Buster has defended her.
jokes.punchline: After a beat, Buster suggests calling her — as if the solution to everything is to involve Lucille more.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'Maybe we should call her' is essential — it's a beat that lets the absurdity sink in. Buster's codependence is so extreme that his immediate reaction to any problem is 'call Mom.' Three words do enormous character work. Economy score near perfect.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael: 'Is this a business decision, or is it personal? 'Cause if it's business, I'll go away happily. But if it's personal, I'll go away. But I won't be happy.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob he can't stay at the office either.
jokes.punchline: Gob offers a Godfather-style distinction between business and personal — then reveals the outcome is the same either way. The 'distinction' is meaningless.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in comedic false choice. Gob sets up a distinction that implies different consequences — the Godfather allusion primes you for drama — and then reveals the outcome is identical either way. The only variable is his emotional state, which Michael clearly doesn't care about. 'I'll go away. But I won't be happy.' is a perfect kicker. High quotability. Earns 8+ for structural elegance and the subverted expectation.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It's personal.' — then a long pause while Gob stares at him.
jokes.setup: Gob just said if it's personal he'll leave unhappily.
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'It's personal' without hesitation — then the pause as Gob absorbs being personally rejected by his own brother.
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to joke 27. Michael answers without a beat of hesitation, making it sting. The subsequent pause earns its laugh because of what was established in the setup — Gob asked for it and got it. Economy is 10/10: two words do all the work.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I'm surprised you tried out for this.' Maeby: 'Yeah. I just love the theater.' [immediately] 'I'm just doing it to kiss Steve Holt. Well actually, I think I'm gonna quit. Yeah, theater's dead.'
jokes.setup: George Michael asks why Maeby auditioned. She gives a sincere-sounding answer.
jokes.punchline: She immediately contradicts it — 'I'm just doing it to kiss Steve Holt' — then pivots to quitting, then reverses when she remembers football will keep Steve busy.
jokes.explanation: Maeby's stream-of-consciousness reversal — sincere, then cynical, then planning dropout, then recommitting based entirely on boy logic — is excellent character work. 'Theater's dead' is a funny throwaway that shows her willingness to declare anything dead when convenient. The rapid reversals in under 15 seconds is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Tobias makes a dramatic entrance: 'Hello, everyone, and welcome to the theater. I am Dr. Tobias Fünke, and I will be your new director.' — long stunned pause from students.
jokes.setup: Maeby's father shows up at her school audition, embarrassing her. The students expect a normal school director.
jokes.punchline: Tobias makes a grandiose theatrical entrance, fully costumed in the role of 'Director' — and the audience can only imagine the students' horrified silence.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias cringe. His insistence on the full title 'Dr. Tobias Fünke' and the theatrical welcome to a high school gym is perfectly in character. The reaction beat (Maeby's 'Oh my God') is the payoff.

jokes.text: Tobias pitching himself to the principal: 'And you tell me you've got some P.E. teacher directing? That just makes me wanna puke all over your head, sir.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is making his case to the school principal for the directing job.
jokes.punchline: 'That just makes me wanna puke all over your head, sir' — an incredibly aggressive and visceral image used in what should be a professional appeal, delivered as if it's a perfectly normal thing to say.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of professional context (job pitch) with visceral aggression ('puke all over your head, sir') is genuinely surprising and characteristically Tobias — he's so devoted to the theatrical that he expresses passion through completely inappropriate imagery. 'Sir' at the end is a perfect formal kicker that makes it even more absurd. Earns 8 for originality and the precision of 'sir.'

jokes.text: Tobias's appeal to the principal in pseudo-Shakespearean verse: 'Give me a chance to tell the Bard's tale / and I give you my word on humble knee / whence you shall not say it wasn't e'er to be.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to convince the principal to give him the directing job.
jokes.punchline: He breaks into pseudo-iambic verse that barely scans — archaic words ('whence,' 'e'er') deployed with theatrical commitment and zero coherence.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's unearned confidence in his pseudo-Shakespearean pitch — and the principal apparently accepting it — is funny. The verse is deliberately bad in a specific way that only Tobias would construct. Solid Tobias character comedy.

jokes.text: Principal, after Tobias's Shakespeare pitch: 'Jerry, you cool with this?' Jerry: 'Sure. Let the little fruit do it.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just made an earnest, overwrought case for himself.
jokes.punchline: The principal's casual aside to Jerry — who dismisses it with a slur that also perfectly describes Tobias — and the job is handed over in the most anticlimactic way possible.
jokes.explanation: The anticlimactic approval — after Tobias's theatrical presentation — deflates his pretension perfectly. 'Let the little fruit do it' functions as both a slur (dark, AD's edge) and an accurate character description, which is specific to this show's layered Tobias comedy. The casualness is funny.

jokes.text: Buster attempting to handle a business call for Michael. Extended sequence of Buster failing at basic phone communication — 'We're excited. We want your business... And, uh, everybody at the Bluth Company... Here I go again.'
jokes.setup: Michael has put Buster to work on the phone.
jokes.punchline: Buster can't get through a single sentence without losing his train of thought — 'Here I go again' is his attempt to restart, implying this has happened multiple times.
jokes.explanation: 'Here I go again' as a self-encouragement mid-train-of-thought is very good Buster — it implies he's been trying multiple times and needs to rally himself each time. The cringe of watching Buster attempt professional phone communication is well-established in a few short lines.

jokes.text: Buster in the office: 'Mom told me to stay away from microwaves.' Then continues: 'And besides, this is gonna be the most bitchingly fast bike in all of Orange County.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Buster he can't do something in the snack room (implied he's using the microwave inappropriately or it's unclear what he's doing).
jokes.punchline: Buster's first defense is 'Mom told me to stay away from microwaves' — an inexplicable maternal prohibition. Then he pivots to enthusiastic pride about the bike using 'bitchingly.'
jokes.explanation: 'Mom told me to stay away from microwaves' is beautifully unexplained — we never learn why, which is funnier than any explanation. It implies Lucille has issued specific appliance-related prohibitions to Buster like he's a child. 'Bitchingly fast' is a great Buster word — enthusiastic, slightly wrong. Both details do perfect character work. Earns 8+ for the unexplained specificity of the microwave prohibition.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Your son is gonna love it.' Michael: 'Well, I guess 3:40 is as good a time as any to call it a day.'
jokes.setup: Buster has declared the bike done and good.
jokes.punchline: Michael, clearly desperate to end the workday with Buster, decides 3:40 PM is an acceptable quitting time — a defeated capitulation.
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Michael's defeated acceptance of early quitting communicates the Buster experience without belaboring it. Solid but unremarkable.

jokes.text: Tobias directing the play: 'You are playing adults with fully formed libidos... not two young men playing grab-ass in the shower.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is directing and stops the rehearsal because the actors aren't playing the romantic tension correctly.
jokes.punchline: 'Not two young men playing grab-ass in the shower' — Tobias's chosen negative example is strangely specific and revealing.
jokes.explanation: Pure Tobias. The specificity of 'grab-ass in the shower' as his counterexample to romantic acting is one of the show's signature Tobias-says-something-inadvertently-gay jokes. It's well-executed because the specificity (not just 'two men playing around' but grab-ass specifically, in a shower specifically) is what makes it transgressive and funny. The direction itself is also bad — telling actors NOT to be like that still reveals his subconscious. Earns 8+ for the perfectly chosen specificity and high character integration.

jokes.text: Tobias sees Michael arrive and immediately announces himself: 'Would you hold the work, please? Oh, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is in the middle of directing when he spots Michael.
jokes.punchline: He stops rehearsal to acknowledge Michael's arrival, prioritizing social interaction over his new professional role.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene transition beat. 'Hold the work' is nice theater-director lingo that shows Tobias has absorbed the vocabulary if not the skills. Unremarkable but not weak.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I'm not [in the play]. I quit.' Maeby: 'George Michael!' — then immediately: 'Wait, no. I love the theater and I gave my word, so I'm back in.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Michael is in the play.
jokes.punchline: George Michael quits and re-enrolls in the space of two lines — driven entirely by proximity to Maeby. His principles are entirely governed by his crush.
jokes.explanation: Mirrors Maeby's earlier quit/recommit (joke 29) with identical logic — the parallel structure rewards attention. George Michael's 'I gave my word' is a thin rationalization for 'Maeby noticed me.' Character-specific.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Why, because of the play? You're just a stand-in.' George Michael: 'No, no. It's not the play. It's just... you know, I can't go on a million bike rides whenever you want me to.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is refusing to go on a bike ride with his dad.
jokes.punchline: Michael dismisses the play as a reason — 'You're just a stand-in' — which is both accurate and accidentally hurtful. Then George Michael overreacts as if they go on 'a million' bike rides.
jokes.explanation: 'A million bike rides' is slightly funny because they've had exactly one bike ride plan all episode. The father-son cringe of Michael accidentally belittling his son ('just a stand-in') while trying to be casual is functional.

jokes.text: Tobias to Michael about George Michael: 'Well, they're different people every day, teenagers. Hormones all ablaze. Except, of course, when they're onstage when I need it.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is reassuring Michael about George Michael's teen behavior.
jokes.punchline: He pivots to his own directorial frustration — teenagers' hormones are great unless they're onstage when he needs them for a play.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's self-centered pivot — turning Michael's parental concern into a complaint about his directing — is pure character. 'Hormones all ablaze' is a funny construction. The interruption of his own advice is well-timed.

jokes.text: Tobias realizes George Michael isn't interested in a girl — his reaction is 'Not a girl? ... Of course.' Then: 'Methinks a Cupid I shall play.'
jokes.setup: Michael wonders if George Michael is pulling away — 'He said it wasn't about a girl.'
jokes.punchline: Tobias has a realization moment ('Of course.') that implies he thinks George Michael is gay — then announces 'Methinks a Cupid I shall play' — while going to pair George Michael with Steve Holt.
jokes.explanation: A dense joke: Tobias (who is almost certainly gay himself) immediately projects onto George Michael, then meddles in a way that would actually be about a girl (Maeby) — Tobias is wrong in all directions simultaneously. 'Methinks a Cupid I shall play' is wonderfully overwrought. Earns 8+ for the layered irony and the rewatch bonus — knowing where this goes makes it richer.

jokes.text: George Sr. in prison: 'I couldn't break away from the poker game. Capital G was down to his boxers.' Michael: 'Strip poker?' George Sr.: 'Yeah, and it's tough. We can really only play about two hands.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is apologizing for missing Michael's visit — he was playing poker.
jokes.punchline: Strip poker in prison is already dark; 'we can only play about two hands' — meaning the men only have enough clothing for two rounds — with 'hands' as both poker terminology and clothing quantity.
jokes.explanation: 'Two hands' works as both poker lingo and clothing logic — a clean double meaning that doesn't require a wink. The visual of prisoners in boxer shorts playing card games is also darkly funny. Strong economy. 'Capital G' as a prison nickname is a nice throwaway.

jokes.text: George Sr. explains Buster's difficult birth: 'Maybe it was the 11 months he spent in the womb. The doctor said there were claw marks on the walls of her uterus, but he was our miracle baby.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is musing about what made Buster the way he is.
jokes.punchline: 'The doctor said there were claw marks on the walls of her uterus' — a horrifyingly specific detail delivered as casual reminiscence.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The image of an infant clawing the uterine walls — implying Buster was literally trying to claw his way BACK IN — is simultaneously horrifying and perfectly explanatory of his attachment to Lucille. The specificity ('claw marks on the walls of her uterus') is what makes it transcend absurdism into something that feels like real mythology. 'But he was our miracle baby' delivered as comfort after that image is a perfect deadpan kicker. Earns the high score because it's genuinely original, structurally perfect, deeply character-integrated (it explains Buster's entire pathology), and quotable.

jokes.text: George Sr., after a long rambling story about Buster: 'Wow. We're just blowing through nap time, aren't we?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been giving Michael philosophical prison wisdom about parenting.
jokes.punchline: He breaks off to note they've run past prison nap time — abruptly returning the scene to the reality that he's an inmate with a schedule.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed deflation of the dramatic scene. 'Nap time' in prison is an unexpected detail — prisons have nap time apparently, or George Sr. calls it that — and noting they've 'blown through' it is charmingly casual. Strong economy: six words cut a pretentious scene back to earth. Earns 8 for the precision of the deflation.

jokes.text: Lindsay hides the dress in the attic crawl space — but the audience sees her emerging and getting positive reactions, implying she's been wearing it around the house. Visual: Lindsay in an elaborate dress crawling into an attic.
jokes.setup: Narrator says Lindsay was getting positive reactions to the dress and decided to hide it.
jokes.punchline: The visual of Lindsay climbing into a crawl space in a formal dress — and the logic that she's hiding it from Michael while wearing it — is physically absurd.
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Lindsay in a dress climbing into a crawl space sets up the later payoff where Tobias finds the dress there. The rewatch bonus is high because the dress's journey is a background running gag. The visual is strong.

jokes.text: Lindsay, meanwhile, still had the dress and she was starting to get some positive reactions to it. / Look at you. / I am.
jokes.setup: The narrator notes Lindsay is getting 'positive reactions' to the dress she shoplifted on the company card.
jokes.punchline: The only visible reaction is Lindsay herself saying 'Look at you' (to herself in a mirror) and responding 'I am' — she's complimenting herself. The 'positive reactions' are entirely self-directed.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's irony is perfectly set up for Lindsay's mirror vanity. The joke is in the juxtaposition between the neutral-sounding 'positive reactions' and the revelation that she's talking to herself. 'Look at you. / I am.' is a compact masterpiece of narcissism — two lines that completely define Lindsay's character. High character integration.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Are you crazy? I'm a magician. I make my living with my hands. And if I'm going to be staying here...' Michael: 'Staying here?'
jokes.setup: Unclear what Gob is reacting to, but he's protecting his hands in the context of the model home.
jokes.punchline: Gob slips in 'if I'm going to be staying here' — as if the question of whether he's staying has already been settled — prompting Michael's flat disbelief.
jokes.explanation: Gob's casual assumption of residence where he hasn't been invited is consistent character behavior. Michael's 'Staying here?' is a good reaction beat. 'I make my living with my hands' is a solid character reminder — Gob takes his magic extremely seriously even when everything else collapses.

jokes.text: Michael: 'What, did that Mexican throw you out?' Gob: 'She's not that Mexican, Mom. She's my Mexican. And she's Colombian or something.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Marta threw Gob out, using the casual dismissal 'that Mexican.'
jokes.punchline: Gob defensively corrects 'that Mexican' to 'my Mexican' — asserting possessiveness over her nationality label rather than her name — then admits he doesn't know where she's from anyway.
jokes.explanation: Gob's defense is perfectly wrong — he corrects the possessive but not the nationality label, then admits he doesn't know if she's even Mexican. The joke reveals layers of Gob: defensive, possessive, but fundamentally incurious about the person he's dating. 'Colombian or something' is the kicker — three words that annihilate any pretense of the correction being principled. Earns 8+ for the structural beauty of his correction making him look worse.

jokes.text: Gob: 'If I can't find a horny immigrant by then, I don't deserve to stay here.'
jokes.setup: Michael gives Gob three days to sort out his situation.
jokes.punchline: Gob sets his own success metric: finding 'a horny immigrant' — which is both offensive and reveals his complete social moral baseline.
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-imposed metric is darkly funny in its specificity and implies a worldview where this is a totally normal Plan B. 'I don't deserve to stay here' as the self-deprecating kicker is funnier than a straight brag — it implies minimum standards he might not meet. Classic Gob.

jokes.text: Tobias to Michael about Steve Holt (now playing Beatrice): 'I wish I could say the same for Steve Holt. I don't know what the hell her problem is.' Michael: 'Her?' Tobias: 'Oh, uh, I'm sorry. I say that because I switched the parts.'
jokes.setup: Tobias refers to Steve Holt as 'her.' Michael is confused.
jokes.punchline: Tobias explains it's because he switched the parts — but the explanation still sounds strange because Tobias is so invested in Steve Holt being female for the play.
jokes.explanation: The confusion is well-structured and Michael's 'Her?' is a perfect reaction beat. Tobias's explanation is both accurate and makes no sense to Michael. Good running thread.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Perhaps if she dressed as a woman.' Tobias: 'She? Oh, for the love of God. She's playing a woman.'
jokes.setup: Michael is confused about Steve Holt's gender in the play and suggests dressing 'her' as a woman.
jokes.punchline: Tobias explodes — 'She's playing a woman!' — because in his mind the casting is perfectly logical and Michael is the confused one.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's exasperation ('Oh, for the love of God') is funny because he's explaining a situation he created and it's still confusing everyone. Michael's innocent suggestion accidentally makes complete sense and somehow makes Tobias angrier. Good escalation of the Steve Holt casting gag.

jokes.text: Lindsay returns home: 'I've been going to the thrift store, but I'm going to find a way to keep that dress.' Things didn't work out with Lucille: 'It was utterly macabre.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been trying to hide her expensive dress from Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'It was utterly macabre' — Lindsay applies the most dramatic possible adjective to what was likely a brief, awkward conversation with her mother.
jokes.explanation: 'Utterly macabre' is a delicious overreaction — the word choice is theatrical and grandiose. Lindsay describing an interaction with her mother as 'macabre' tells us everything about the Bluth family dynamic while being funny in its excess.

jokes.text: I'm exhausted. / Neiman's again, huh?
jokes.setup: Lindsay returns home looking tired.
jokes.punchline: Gob's immediate assumption is that she's been shopping at Neiman Marcus — which is apparently so well-established as Lindsay's activity that it's the first guess. It also implies Gob knows his sister very well.
jokes.explanation: Economical character joke — Neiman's as Lindsay's default exhaustion source. The joke is in the familiarity of Gob's guess. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'She always makes everything about her. She's the last person I ever want to need something from. She gets off on being withholding.' — said while Buster is clearly in the room.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is complaining about Lucille to Tobias.
jokes.punchline: Buster — Lucille's most devoted child — is in the room absorbing every word.
jokes.explanation: The cringe element — Buster hearing his mother described this way — sets up his explosion. Functional scene-building rather than a punchline joke.

jokes.text: Buster finally erupts: 'Whoa. Buster.' / 'I'm Mom, and I wanna shoot down everything you say so I feel good about myself.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias have been complaining about Lucille in front of Buster.
jokes.punchline: Buster — usually passive and deferential — gives a surprisingly accurate impression of Lucille's psychology: 'I wanna shoot down everything you say so I feel good about myself.'
jokes.explanation: Buster's Lucille impression is unexpectedly incisive — he has been observing her longer than anyone and understands her psychology exactly. The comedy comes from both the accuracy of the impression and the source (Buster, who normally adores her). The others' 'Look who's ragging on the old lady' / 'Finally' shows the family's shared experience. Earns 8+ for the character reveal and the satisfying payoff of Buster's pent-up resentment.

jokes.text: Lindsay/Tobias reaction: 'Well, no one's gonna top that.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just called his mother a 'horny slut.'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay delivers the perfect meta-acknowledgment: 'Well, no one's gonna top that.' — recognizing the moment for what it is.
jokes.explanation: The button to the Buster eruption. Six words, perfectly placed, that let the audience breathe. Lindsay's deadpan acknowledgment of the extremity functions almost as a meta-commentary on the joke itself. Earns its score for being the ideal button on the episode's best joke.

jokes.text: Tobias finds the red dress Lindsay hid in the attic crawl space and puts it on Steve Holt for the play.
jokes.setup: Lindsay hid her dress in the attic crawl space. Tobias needed a costume for his female lead Steve Holt.
jokes.punchline: Tobias found and used the dress — paying off the crawl-space hiding — and now Steve Holt is wearing Lindsay's expensive stolen dress as a theatrical costume.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful convergence of two running storylines — the dress subplot and the Shakespeare play subplot — coming together in a way that serves both. The narrator's announcement of the dress being found sets up Lindsay's subsequent phone call to Lucille. Classic AD plot-braid payoff. Earns 8+ for the structural satisfaction of multiple threads resolving together.

jokes.text: Lindsay calls Lucille for money for a new dress. Lucille immediately: 'Get a job.' Then Lindsay invents a story that Maeby was beaten up and had her clothes stolen. Lucille: 'Send her over. I'll take her shopping.' Buster: 'You get off on being withholding, don't you?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks Lucille for money. Lucille refuses. Lindsay lies about Maeby being attacked.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately agrees to help the (fictional) victimized grandchild — revealing she IS capable of generosity, but only in response to tragedy and manipulation. Buster then delivers a pointed callback to the earlier 'gets off on withholding' line.
jokes.explanation: Three-part joke with escalating payoffs: 'Get a job' (Lucille's reflex rejection), Lucille's instant reversal for a sob story (proving Lindsay right about manipulation being the only language Lucille speaks), and Buster's callback landing perfectly. The structure validates Lindsay's cynicism while making Lucille's behavior legible as comedy.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Buster said that? My Buster?' Lindsay: 'Not anymore, apparently.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is shocked that Buster — the child most devoted to her — said she 'gets off on being withholding.'
jokes.punchline: 'Not anymore, apparently' — Lindsay delivers a quietly devastating update on Buster's loyalty status.
jokes.explanation: Three words ('Not anymore, apparently') do enormous work — confirming Buster's rebellion, delivering it to Lucille, and satisfying the episode's character arc. Economy score is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Callback: Lucille arrives at the model home and we cut to Buster, who says 'Zip me up.' — the third use of 'Zip me up' in the episode, but now Buster saying it to someone else.
jokes.setup: The episode has featured 'Zip me up' from Lucille and Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: Now Buster says it — cementing it as a Bluth-family behavioral tic, and showing that Buster has also absorbed this demanding helplessness.
jokes.explanation: The third 'Zip me up' closes the running gag. Each instance recontextualizes the family — Lucille does it to Buster (control), Lindsay does it to Tobias (entitlement), Buster does it to Michael (learned helplessness). Three words carry a complete character commentary. This is AD's structural sophistication in miniature. Earns 8.5 for the formal elegance of the running gag's completion.

jokes.text: Buster built the bike but 'neglected to build the brakes.' Buster: 'I'm flying!'
jokes.setup: Buster proudly built the bike, claiming it would be the fastest in Orange County.
jokes.punchline: He forgot the brakes — a catastrophic but completely Buster oversight — and is now hurtling out of control screaming 'I'm flying!'
jokes.explanation: The perfect payoff to Buster building the bike. His pride ('most bitchingly fast bike in Orange County') made us forget to worry about the build quality — and then the narrator drops the brake revelation immediately before showing the consequence. 'I'm flying!' in this context is both accurate and a great ironic line. Earns 8+ for structural payoff and character-specific incompetence — only Buster would be thrilled while careening out of control.

jokes.text: George Michael quits the play, then learns Maeby has now been cast as a man. Maeby: 'So you want me to play the man?' Tobias: 'Yes. Don't you see? I cast the parts all wrong. It was Daddy's fault.'
jokes.setup: The play's casting has been reshuffled: George Michael is out, Maeby is now playing the male part, Steve Holt is Beatrice.
jokes.punchline: Tobias announces 'It was Daddy's fault' with theatrical self-flagellation — but the entire mess IS Tobias's fault, so this is accidentally correct.
jokes.explanation: 'It was Daddy's fault' is rich because Tobias says it as dramatic self-criticism while being perfectly accurate — it IS Daddy's fault. His theatrical self-blame is indistinguishable from his normal theatrical delivery. The escalation of the casting chaos is satisfying.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I still get to kiss Steve, right?' Tobias: 'It's gonna be a huge disaster. I'll get you tickets.'
jokes.setup: Maeby asks if she still gets to kiss Steve Holt despite the reversed casting.
jokes.punchline: Tobias, unprompted, predicts the play will be 'a huge disaster' and offers Maeby tickets — acknowledging the disaster while still planning to proceed.
jokes.explanation: 'It's gonna be a huge disaster. I'll get you tickets.' is one of the episode's best lines. Tobias predicts failure with complete calm and then immediately pivots to offering front-row seats — as if the disaster is a show worth watching. It's self-aware and self-deluded simultaneously. The economy is perfect — two short sentences do everything. Earns 8+ for quotability, economy, and character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille arrives at the model home with a candy bar for Buster: 'Oh... No. I'm withholding it. Look at me getting off.'
jokes.setup: Lucille shows up, offers Buster a candy bar — then remembers what he said about her getting off on withholding.
jokes.punchline: She demonstrates the behavior in real time — 'No. I'm withholding it. Look at me getting off.' — fully self-aware in the worst possible way.
jokes.explanation: Among the best jokes in the episode and possibly the season. Lucille arrives with a peace offering, then demonstrates the exact pathology Buster described — withholding the candy bar — and NARRATES HER OWN BEHAVIOR: 'Look at me getting off.' This is Lucille at maximum — she's not unconscious of the manipulation, she's delighted by it. The joke is both the behavior and the self-commentary. 'Look at me getting off' is one of the most quotable AD lines in memory. Earns 9 for structural perfection, character integration (only Lucille), earned payoff of the episode's Lucille arc, and quotability.

jokes.text: Lucille to Michael: 'I'm not going to let you destroy my relationship with my son just because you don't have one with yours right now.'
jokes.setup: Lucille accuses Michael of turning Buster against her.
jokes.punchline: She uses the parenting accusation against Michael as a weapon — 'you don't have one with yours right now' — which is also true, making it sting.
jokes.explanation: Lucille weaponizing accurate insight is classic Lucille. It's more dramatically functional than comedic, but there's dark humor in how efficiently she identifies and exploits Michael's actual fear.

jokes.text: Michael tells Buster he can make his own decisions. Buster: 'Michael. I wanna go with Mom.' Michael: 'You said that she shoots you down.' Buster: 'I was trying to fit in.'
jokes.setup: Michael appeals to Buster's self-determination — giving him the choice to stay.
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately chooses Lucille. When pressed on his previous complaints, he admits he was just 'trying to fit in' — all his rebellion was performative.
jokes.explanation: 'I was trying to fit in.' is one of the best lines in the episode and possibly the season. The entire episode has built toward Buster's liberation from Lucille — and it turns out none of it was real. He was performing rebellion because the others were doing it. This retroactively recontextualizes every moment of his 'rebellion' as social mimicry, which is both devastatingly funny and quietly sad. The reversal is complete and perfectly earned. Earns 8.5+ for structural payoff, character revelation, and the specific phrase 'trying to fit in' which captures Buster's pathology in four words.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You were flying today, buddy.' Buster: 'Yes, I was flying... but a little too close to the sun.' Lucille: 'You let him go in the sun? Get in the backseat. I'll be right out.'
jokes.setup: Michael uses 'flying' metaphorically — Buster was free today. Buster echoes with the Icarus metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Lucille interprets 'flying...close to the sun' completely literally and panics about UV exposure — 'You let him go in the sun?' — collapsing the metaphor into maternal overprotection.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments. The Icarus metaphor is being used earnestly — Michael and Buster having a genuine poetic moment — and Lucille pulverizes it with literal-mindedness about sunscreen. 'You let him go in the sun?' perfectly captures Lucille's inability to see Buster as anything but a fragile object to protect from weather. The structural elegance is exceptional: metaphor built and destroyed in three lines. 'Get in the backseat. I'll be right out.' as the kicker confirms she's already taking control. Earns 9 for the literary allusion's destruction, character integration, and the multi-level satisfaction of the joke.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Front seat, Mom. I sit in the front seat now. Mmm?'
jokes.setup: Lucille has told Buster to get in the backseat.
jokes.punchline: Buster asserts front-seat privileges with 'Mmm?' — a tiny act of defiance that is simultaneously pathetic (he's asserting a child's status symbol) and triumphant for him.
jokes.explanation: 'I sit in the front seat now. Mmm?' is perfect Buster — his rebellion has been completely absorbed back into submission, but he reserves one tiny victory: the front seat. The 'Mmm?' is the chef's kiss — a completely unthreatening challenge, like a toddler standing up to a parent. The specificity of the front seat as his line in the sand is both funny and perfectly in-scale with Buster's stunted development.

jokes.text: George Michael watches Maeby kiss Steve Holt onstage — the kiss he wanted — being performed by the person he wanted to kiss, with the person he was almost forced to kiss.
jokes.setup: George Michael wanted to kiss Maeby; Maeby wanted to kiss Steve Holt; Tobias's casting created a situation where George Michael almost kissed Steve Holt.
jokes.punchline: George Michael watches from the audience as Maeby and Steve Holt share the kiss — he gets none of what he wanted, the entire subplot resolves against him.
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of George Michael watching the exact kiss he wanted, going to the wrong person, in a play he left — is structurally satisfying and dark. The narrator's framing ('the kiss that should've been his, with a boy he almost had to kiss') makes the irony explicit. Rewatch bonus for the layered callback.

jokes.text: Maeby after kissing Steve Holt: 'You smell like my mom.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has been desperately pursuing the chance to kiss Steve Holt all episode.
jokes.punchline: After finally getting it, her only response is 'You smell like my mom' — a deflating callback to Lindsay's perfume/the dress subplot.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. The entire episode has been building to Maeby kissing Steve Holt — it's her motivation from the first scene. The payoff is 'You smell like my mom.' which simultaneously: (1) ends the romantic subplot with maximum anticlimax, (2) pays off the dress subplot (Steve is wearing Lindsay's dress), (3) connects everything. Five words close out multiple storylines. The economy is perfect — '10' is justified because you could not do this with fewer words. The structural elegance of this callback earns its high score.

jokes.text: George Michael is in the kitchen waiting when Michael comes home. They decide to make cornballs together — and the Cornballer immediately burns them both.
jokes.setup: The episode opened with Michael and George Michael at the Cornballer. It ends the same way.
jokes.punchline: The structural callback — father and son reconciled, back at the Cornballer — is sweet, then immediately undercut by another burn: 'Watch your... Mother of God! Oh! Every damn time!'
jokes.explanation: The structural symmetry is elegant — the episode opens and closes on the same gag. 'Every damn time!' is perfect because it implies this has happened before, will happen again, and nothing will change. The reconciliation being immediately undercut by another burn is very AD — sentimentality punctured by the same recurring obstacle. The full Cornballer running gag reaches its closing note. 'Every damn time!' is a great kicker line. Earns 8.5 for structural closure and the emotional resonance of the callback.

jokes.text: Tag: Tobias gets a review of his Shakespeare play. He reads: 'I didn't get into this business to please sophomore Tracy Schwarzman' — then 'onward and upward!' — then breaks down crying: 'Why, Tracy? Why?'
jokes.setup: Narrator promises Tobias gets a review of his play.
jokes.punchline: He defiantly reads the bad review ('I didn't get into this business to please...'), declares 'onward and upward!' — then immediately collapses: 'Why, Tracy? Why?'
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure is perfect: defiance → declaration → collapse. Tobias's bravado ('I didn't get into this business to please...') evaporates within seconds. 'Why, Tracy? Why?' directed at a high school sophomore who gave him a bad review is devastating and funny. The name 'Tracy Schwarzman' is a great detail — specific enough to be real, odd enough to be funny. Earns 8+ for the escalation structure and Tobias's complete lack of resilience.

jokes.text: Final tag: 'And Buster is back where he belongs. Mom! What is it? I can't get down.'
jokes.setup: The episode has been about Buster potentially gaining independence.
jokes.punchline: The final image: Buster stranded somewhere, calling for his mom — back exactly where he started, if not worse. The episode's arc is zero.
jokes.explanation: The perfect closing joke for Buster's arc. After 'I sit in the front seat now,' he is immediately stuck somewhere helpless, calling for mom. The narrator's 'And Buster is back where he belongs' delivers the irony efficiently. A great structural button on the episode's B-plot.

jokes.text: Tag: Buster is back with Lucille. Lucille: 'What is it?' Buster: 'I can't get down.'
jokes.setup: Narrator says 'Buster is back where he belongs.' — implying resolution.
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately needs help getting down from somewhere (implied: somewhere he climbed that Lucille's excessive care has made him unable to handle alone), and says 'I can't get down.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing tag for Buster's episode arc. 'Buster is back where he belongs' sets up triumphant resolution — then 'I can't get down' delivers the darkest possible version: Buster needs Lucille for even basic tasks. It confirms that nothing has changed and nothing will. 'I can't get down' as the episode's final Buster line is both funny and quietly devastating. Earns 8+ for structural closure and character thematic resonance.

tmdb_overview: Michael spends quality time with Buster. George Michael auditions for a school play to get closer to Maeby. Gob breaks up with his girlfriend and has nowhere to go.
insight: Buster's neediness spirals into 70 jokes across 22 minutes—character comedy at peak efficiency.
